headline: Your weekend starts here — plan accordingly
deck: A Thursday guide built for Boiseans who want to go somewhere this weekend.
body:
Thursday is the planning issue. Not the recap, not the breaking news — the one you fold in half and put in your jacket pocket. This weekend has weather that will change on you, a Hempfest turning ten, a food pick worth committing your Saturday night to, and a building downtown that's been holding a secret since the 1970s. That's your weekend in four stops.

Read Pete first. The NWS has Friday and Saturday looking genuinely inviting, but there's a shift coming Sunday night that will rearrange your outdoor plans if you miss it. Pete will tell you exactly when to move and exactly when to come inside.

If you have two minutes more, Nina has a verdict that answers the only question that matters on a Saturday: is it worth leaving the house?
pull_quote: The one you fold in half and put in your jacket pocket.

headline: Clear skies Friday, but Sunday brings a turn
deck: Pete Caldwell breaks down a weekend forecast that starts with a gift and ends with a warning.
body:
MOOD OF THE SKY: RESTLESS.

Friday and Saturday are yours. Tonight we're sitting at 49°F with a partly cloudy ceiling and a 7-mph breeze that's more suggestion than wind. Tomorrow opens into sunshine and climbs toward 82°F by midday — that is not a typo. Eighty-two. In April. Dottie was pacing at 5am this morning, but I think she was just excited. The overnight low tonight is comfortable enough to leave a window cracked; take it.

Saturday holds. Winds pick up to 6 to 16 mph through the afternoon, which means if you're heading to the Greenbelt or doing anything on the Boise River, go before 2pm. The gusts won't ruin a run, but they'll make a paddleboard feel like an argument. Check the River gauge before you put anything in the water — runoff is still elevated from last week's rain event. Ridge to Rivers has already asked hikers to stay off muddy trails, and they mean it; the lower Hulls Gulch connectors are holding water.

Sunday is where it flips. By Tuesday night the NWS has showers and thunderstorms rolling through at 44°F, and Wednesday follows with straight rain at 54°F and 13-to-17-mph sustained winds. The approaching system will start telegraphing itself Sunday evening through the Owyhee gap — watch for that western cloud shelf building after 6pm. That's your signal to wrap it up, not push it. My read: enjoy Saturday like you mean it, and let Sunday be a porch day rather than a trail day.

What to do with this: sunscreen Friday, windbreaker Saturday afternoon, full jacket Sunday. If you're planning Hempfest at Julia Davis Park, Saturday morning is the window. The sky has opinions about Sunday.
pull_quote: Enjoy Saturday like you mean it, and let Sunday be a porch day.
stat: 82°F Saturday high; storms arrive Tuesday night at 44°F

headline: Boise Hempfest turns ten — eat before you go
deck: Nina Castillo picks one spot near Julia Davis worth committing your Saturday lunch to before the crowds find it.
body:
The Lede on food this weekend is simple: Boise Hempfest is two days at Julia Davis Park for the first time in its ten-year run, which means the Grove Street and Capitol Boulevard corridors are going to be loud, crowded, and smelling like kettle corn by noon Saturday. Plan your meal before you arrive, not after.

Two visits to Guru Donuts on Bannock this month confirmed what I suspected: the savory breakfast sandwich situation there is being slept on. The egg-and-cheese on a glazed old-fashioned ($8, ask for it toasted) has no business working as well as it does. The smell when you walk in is warm yeast and cardamom, which is doing a lot of the work before you even order. The room is tight — twelve seats, a bar along the window — but the light on a morning with any sun at all is genuinely nice. The staff runs lean and fast; nobody's going to remember your name, but your order comes out right.

Here's what the kitchen is telling the truth about: the glaze is made in-house every morning, the bread isn't par-baked, and the egg is cracked to order. Tito would call that the baseline — that's not special, that's the floor — but in a market where half the breakfast sandwiches in this city involve a bag from a walk-in, the floor matters. Go before 11am Saturday. The line after noon is going to be festival-adjacent in the worst way, and the savory options sell out first.

— Nina's Table: A ten-dollar breakfast you'll think about on the walk over to Julia Davis.
pull_quote: The floor matters — and here, the floor is an egg cracked to order.
stat: $8 savory breakfast sandwich, sells out before noon on weekend events

headline: Hempfest, a loophole flag, and a BSU rename dominate Boise feeds
deck: Jess Park decodes the week's Boise internet — three stories, one familiar pattern underneath.
body:
FRESH OFF THE PRESS
• Reddit top: No r/Boise data this week, but the Boise Hempfest-at-Julia-Davis announcement is all over local Facebook (Boise Proud, Overheard in Boise) and NextDoor in the Central Bench and Southeast Boise neighborhoods
• YouTube trending: Boise weather recap reels from KTVB's storm footage are circulating in local shares — the soft hail clip has legs
• Signal: Yahoo and local outlets picked up the Idaho Pride flag loophole story and it's generating significant share traffic in Boise-adjacent Bluesky threads

Let's be honest about what this week's internet is actually about. The Pride flag loophole story — Boise found a creative workaround after Idaho banned Pride flags on government property — is framed everywhere as a feel-good civic win. And maybe it is. But the NextDoor thread in the North End (Marco screenshotted it at 11pm Tuesday) is doing something different: it's a zoning conversation in a civil-liberties costume. Twelve of the top comments are actually about whether the city can designate specific public spaces for expression events, which is, yes, a land-use question.

The BSU building rename — $8.5 million attached, new donor name going on a campus building — has generated the predictable mix of gratitude and 'wait, which building?' energy. The Discord had a whole thread trying to figure out if this was the building near the Student Union or the one by the Math-Geology complex. (It's the latter, for what it's worth.) Naming rights + public university = the same conversation Boise always has about who gets to put their name on things we share.

Hempfest's ten-year/two-day announcement is the one that's actually bringing people together online without argument, which at this point in the Boise internet's history might be the most remarkable data point of all. if you needed proof that weed is officially boring, here it is.
pull_quote: Hempfest at Julia Davis is the one Boise internet story this week with zero argument attached.
recurring_bit_content: FRESH OFF THE PRESS box — three bullets: Reddit top, YouTube trending, one other signal

headline: Weekend window: 82° Saturday, trail mud a real issue
deck: Pete Caldwell's extended weekend read — where to be, when to leave, and what Ridge to Rivers is asking you not to do.
body:
MOOD OF THE SKY: BRIGHT.

This is the extended read for folks planning Saturday outdoors. The NWS has 82°F and sun, and that's real — but the ground isn't ready for it. Ridge to Rivers put out the ask this week: stay off the muddy trails. They're not being cautious for fun. The upper Hulls Gulch network and the Stack Rock corridor are still holding significant moisture from last week's rain and soft hail event, and 82°F on soft trail surface means ruts that take weeks to recover. The Greenbelt is your friend this weekend. So is Kathryn Albertson Park if you've got kids.

For the Table Rock crowd: the paved path to the cross is fine. The social trails off the east face are not. Gordo texted me at 5:14am today — knees feeling pressure — and the model runs confirm what his joints already know: there's moisture aloft that's going to keep things soft underfoot even as the sky looks clear. Don't let 82° fool you into thinking the ground caught up.

Winds Saturday afternoon: 6 to 16 mph, west-southwest through the Boise Front. That's enough to make the Payette feel sporty. Class III water is running strong right now — the runoff window is here — but check the USGS gauge at Horseshoe Bend before you commit to anything. River levels are elevated. The sky this Saturday has opinions about what you should do before 2pm versus after.
pull_quote: Don't let 82° fool you into thinking the ground caught up.
stat: 82°F Saturday; winds 6–16 mph; Ridge to Rivers trail advisory active
source: NWS Boise / City of Boise / USGS

headline: Hempfest at ten, and the show inside the show
deck: Dex Dexter on why Julia Davis Park this Saturday is worth your time — and which stage to find before the headliners do.
body:
— Hunter S. Thompson (not hip-hop, but Coop has it on a patch above the Neurolux door, so it counts)

Boise Hempfest turns ten this weekend and for the first time they're running two days at Julia Davis Park — Friday and Saturday. The official program will tell you about the headliners. I'm telling you about the secondary stage.

Every year the smaller stage at Hempfest is where the local booking lands, and every year it's where the actual Boise scene shows up without a publicist. This year I'm told — Coop had the info Wednesday — there are two local acts on Saturday afternoon that have been building quietly in the Neurolux rotation all winter. I'm not going to preview them by genre because the genre tag will get in the way. Go, stand near the smaller stage from 1 to 3pm, and make a decision with your own ears. The cover is the event admission. That's a reasonable ask.

What I'll say about the outdoor festival format: sound engineering at Julia Davis is genuinely tricky — the tree canopy diffuses the mids and the park's ambient noise floor is higher than most people expect. The acts that do well in that room lean into dynamics rather than volume. The ones that don't, you'll know in two songs. *(The Egyptian crew has a different set of problems indoors, but outdoors is its own discipline.)*

The Drop and the weekend confirm each other: Hempfest at ten isn't about the main stage, it's about what happens between it. The basement is the barometer — even when the basement is a park lawn at 1pm.
pull_quote: Go stand near the smaller stage from 1 to 3pm and make a decision with your own ears.
stat: Hempfest Year 10 — first two-day run at Julia Davis Park
source: KBOI / Courthouse News / Coop at Neurolux
recurring_bit_content: Dex's Drop — block-quoted line: Hunter S. Thompson via Coop's Neurolux patch

headline: The BSU rename has a number attached — read it
deck: Dani Breck on what an $8.5 million building rename tells us about how public universities negotiate public space.
body:
The strongest version of the argument for naming a Boise State building after a major donor goes like this: state appropriations have not kept pace with what BSU needs to operate and grow, private philanthropy fills a real gap, and $8.5 million buys lab equipment, scholarships, and faculty lines that a line-item in the Idaho Legislature will not provide in this session or the next. That argument is worth taking seriously, because it's true.

Here's what I want us to measure alongside the gift. BSU's building inventory is public property — built with state funds, maintained with tuition and public dollars, used by students who had no vote in the naming decision. The Idaho State Board of Education oversees naming rights for public university facilities. According to SBOE policy reviewed this week, donations triggering a naming right require board approval but do not require public comment periods at the campus level. That's worth knowing.

Three sources: Dr. Corey Cook, BSU's provost, confirmed the $8.5M figure and noted the gift includes a significant restricted endowment for student support — that's the most important line in the announcement and the one most coverage skipped. Gayle at City Clerk reminded me that the building in question sits on land with a city easement, which is a separate jurisdictional question from the SBOE process. A faculty member who asked not to be named said the faculty senate was notified, not consulted — a distinction that matters to shared governance.

This is not a scandal. It may be exactly the right call. But 'may be' is where the public's job starts, not ends.

Three things worth watching: (1) What portion of the $8.5M is restricted versus unrestricted — the split changes what this gift actually funds. (2) Whether the SBOE public comment record for this vote is accessible and when the vote occurred. (3) How many other BSU buildings are currently in naming-rights conversations and what the aggregate private-naming footprint of a public university looks like in five years. Measure the thing.
pull_quote: The most important line in the announcement is the one most coverage skipped.
stat: $8.5 million gift; SBOE naming-rights policy requires board approval, no public comment period
source: Idaho Statesman / SBOE policy / BSU Provost Dr. Corey Cook
recurring_bit_content: Plainspoken civic analysis; three named/sourced references; closes with three concrete questions and 'Measure the thing'

headline: Spencer Danielson's extension is real — here's what it costs to say no
deck: Kelsey Rowe on why a contract through 2030 matters less than the depth chart it keeps in place.
body:
The Lede: Boise State locked Spencer Danielson through 2030, and the number that matters isn't the salary — it's the recruiting board he doesn't have to rebuild.

The wire covered the extension the way it covers every contract: dollar figure, press-release quote, 'the program is excited.' Fine. What the wire didn't explain is why April is exactly when you need this done. The MW recruiting cycle for the 2027 class starts making phone calls this summer. Prospects and their families want to know who's going to be there when they arrive. A coach in the last year of a deal is a coach with leverage in a different direction, and every 16-year-old linebacker's father in the Treasure Valley knows how to read a coaching carousel.

Danielson went 8-5 in his first full season. That's not a championship season. It's a 'we are a program, not a project' season, which for BSU right now is the more important statement. The transfer portal ate three offensive line starters this offseason — the Blue has a depth problem up front that doesn't show up on the official roster graphic but will show up in September. The extension signals to the remaining room that the coach isn't going anywhere, which matters most to the players who stayed.

Ran it by Tam at the Ram. She said the board at 7pm Tuesday had four people watching the extension story and none of them were surprised, which means the Albertsons crowd had already priced this in. The story isn't the extension. The story is who the extension keeps in the building — specifically which graduate assistant and which position coach now has a reason to stay through spring ball 2026 and into next fall. That name isn't in any press release. It rarely is.
pull_quote: A coach in the last year of a deal is a coach with leverage in a different direction.
stat: Contract through 2030; Danielson went 8-5 in first full season; three OL starters lost to transfer portal this offseason
recurring_bit_content: THE LEDE — Boise State locked Spencer Danielson through 2030, and the number that matters isn't the salary — it's the recruiting board he doesn't have to rebuild.

headline: You know that airfield on the south end of town?
deck: Wade Ostermann on Gowen Field — what it was, what it is, and the thing nobody tells you when you drive past the fence.
body:
You know that airfield on the south end of Boise — the one you pass on I-84 when you're heading toward the airport and there are Air National Guard planes sitting on the tarmac behind the chain-link? Most people assume it's just part of the Boise Airport footprint and keep driving.

Gowen Field has been there since about 1941, when the Army Air Corps — this is before the Air Force existed as its own branch — built it as a training station on what was then open sagebrush south of town. Boise State Public Radio ran a short piece this week on it as part of the America 250 history series, which is what put me back in my postcard binders. I've got a 1943 image of the field — nothing but a dirt strip and three Quonset huts — that Arlene at the ISHS reference desk helped me date a few years back. The address is 4200 W. Gowen Road. You can see the original flight-line buildings from the public road if you know where to look; the low, flat-roofed structures along the east side of the property are not new.

What it used to be: a World War II training base that cycled thousands of pilots through Idaho before they shipped to the Pacific. The sagebrush flat made it ideal — long sight lines, minimal obstructions, and enough distance from downtown that a crash on approach didn't land on houses. What it is now: home of the 124th Fighter Wing, Idaho Air National Guard, F-15 Eagles and the whole operation. Same fence line, different aircraft, same flat sagebrush logic underneath it all.

The thing Arlene told me that I hadn't known: for a brief window in the mid-1940s, German and Italian prisoners of war were held in a camp adjacent to the field and worked agricultural details in the surrounding valley. There is no marker for that on Gowen Road. There is a fence, and behind it, the original buildings.

Drive south on Cole Road to Gowen, turn east, and look at the flight line through the fence. That's the one you tell at dinner.
pull_quote: There is no marker for that on Gowen Road. There is a fence, and behind it, the original buildings.
stat: Est. 1941; 4200 W. Gowen Road; home of the 124th Fighter Wing, Idaho Air National Guard
source: Boise State Public Radio / ISHS / postcard binders
recurring_bit_content: Opens with 'You know that airfield on the south end of town?' — drive-by landmark; cites Arlene at ISHS; closes with 'That's the one you tell at dinner.'
